metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `.rebar/` overlay schema (key format for project criterion IDs, threshold field type/range, gate-routing field, namespace separator) so two implementors produce the same file; reference or extend the b744 `criteria_routing.json` field set explicitly.
findings.finding: The .rebar/ routing overlay schema — namespace format for project criterion IDs, threshold field structure, and merge semantics — is not defined; an executor must design this from scratch.
findings.location: Design Decisions §6 / AC: 'Project criteria are discovered and explicitly activated via config, with routing/threshold in a .rebar/ overlay (namespaced, additive; presence != active)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a schema snippet (even informal) to Design Decision §6, e.g.: `{"project.no_raw_sql": {"applies_at": ["pass1"], "threshold": 0.7, "blocking": true}}` and state the namespace separator (`.`) and collision rule (any ID matching a built-in key is rejected at load time).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify whether DET invariant scans are scoped to changed files (diff) or the whole repository, and reconcile this with the 'change-scoped (plan/diff)' out-of-scope note.
findings.finding: The Out of Scope section declares 'Baseline/grandfathering — gates are change-scoped (plan/diff), not whole-repo scans,' but Design Decision 2 routes DET invariants through the existing grounding scan facade, which scans file content (not diffs). No section reconciles whether DET invariant scans are diff-scoped or whole-repo.
findings.location: Out of Scope section ('Baseline/grandfathering') vs. Design Decision 2 / AC4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Broaden AC8 (or add a companion criterion) to cover attestation invalidation for all gate types (plan-review and code-review) when a shared project criterion is activated, edited, or disabled.
findings.finding: AC8 scopes attestation invalidation to 'plan-review attestation' only, but the epic's explicit cross-cutting design intent (Shared Registry, Success Criteria 1) means code-review also issues attestations backed by the same shared criteria registry — yet AC8 does not cover code-review attestation invalidation on criteria change.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC8 vs. Context / Success Criteria 1
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'plan-review attestation' in AC8 with 'any gate attestation backed by the shared registry (plan-review, code-review)' or split into AC8a/AC8b per gate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section as an explicit deliverable capturing: unified registry design, overlay merge semantics (additive, last-wins, namespace isolation, can't rebind built-ins), attestation regver/effective-criteria invalidation contract, and cache-key isolation requirement.
findings.finding: The plan introduces several novel cross-cutting architectural decisions (unified criterion registry, overlay merge semantics, attestation regver/effective-criteria extension, cache-key isolation) but calls out no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc as a deliverable — only an informal 'see rationale comment' reference — leaving the decisions undocumented for future gate implementors.
findings.location: Design decisions section; Acceptance Criteria; Out of scope — 'Design decisions (settled in brainstorm; see rationale comment)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'An ADR (or CLAUDE.md section) documents the unified registry contract, overlay merge semantics, and attestation invalidation protocol for downstream gate implementors.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Decompose this epic into child tickets — at minimum: (1) shared registry + loader fix, (2) DET scan wiring, (3) LLM project-criteria activation, (4) eval/calibration CLI surface, (5) attestation invalidation, (6) editor extension + live preview — each child should be independently mergeable and testable.
findings.finding: This is an epic-scale body (10 ACs, ≥6 net-new items across ≥3 independent layers: registry/loader, DET scan wiring, eval/calibration, attestation, editor/UI, routing pipeline) with no child tickets defined. It also mixes UI+backend, includes a registry migration (post-b744 unification), is multi-persona (backend, gate, editor), and exceeds the >6 ACs threshold — all signals that decomposition into children is mandatory, not optional.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (10 ACs) + 'Net-new (build)' section (6+ items)
findings.suggested_fix: Create child tickets with a dependency order: (1) shared registry + cache fix [unblocked after b744]; (2) DET scan adapter [depends on 1]; (3) LLM criteria activation + routing overlay [depends on 1]; (4) attestation invalidation [depends on 2+3]; (5) eval/calibration view [depends on 3]; (6) editor extension + live preview [depends on 3, can parallel 4+5].

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define an explicit thin vertical slice as MVP — e.g., 'one project LLM criterion activates and runs in plan-review (no editor, no eval UI)' — and sequence the remaining work evidence-gated on that MVP landing successfully.
findings.finding: The plan presents all net-new work as a flat, unsequenced list with no thin vertical slice or evidence-gated MVP. There is no identified 'riskiest piece first' — for example, the cache-leak bug in lru_cache loaders (a correctness invariant the whole design depends on) and the claim that 'nothing calls the DET scan consumer today' are high-risk unknowns that should be validated before building the eval UI or editor extension.
findings.location: 'Net-new (build)' section; no 'Sequencing' or 'MVP' section exists
findings.suggested_fix: Proposed MVP: fix the lru_cache key → load one project criterion from .rebar/prompts/ overlay → run it in plan-review → surface finding. Gate the eval/calibration view, DET scan wiring, and editor extension as follow-on children that depend on the MVP attestation passing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the project-criterion ID discovery mechanism: how does `effective_criteria(repo_root)` know which project IDs to include alongside `CANONICAL_LLM` — e.g. enumerate `.rebar/prompts/` entries with `category: criterion` via `enumerate_criteria(repo_root=repo_root)`, or require an explicit activation list in the `.rebar/` routing overlay (presence-in-overlay != active per design decision 6, so activation must be explicit).
findings.finding: The plan says `effective_criteria(repo_root)` is needed to load project criteria, but never specifies how project criterion IDs are DISCOVERED — the current `load_criteria()` iterates only `sorted(CANONICAL_LLM)` (a hardcoded frozenset). A project criterion authored in `.rebar/prompts/` will not appear in this iteration regardless of how the routing overlay is structured, so the gate will silently skip all project criteria unless the ID-discovery mechanism is explicitly specified.
findings.location: ## What exists vs. what we build — 'Net-new (build)' bullet 1: 'Needs `effective_criteria(repo_root)` through the loader + coverage guard'
findings.suggested_fix: The lowest-friction path: in the routing overlay, an `activate: [id1, id2]` list is the explicit activation signal (satisfying 'presence != active'). `effective_criteria(repo_root)` = `load_criteria()` result PLUS each activated project ID resolved via `get_prompt(id, repo_root=repo_root)` + the overlay's routing entry for that ID. This makes discovery O(activated set), not a directory scan, and keeps the packaged `CANONICAL_LLM` frozenset untouched.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend `registry_version()` (or introduce a `project_registry_version(repo_root)` variant) to incorporate the project `.rebar/` routing overlay content so that activating/editing/disabling a project criterion changes the hash and invalidates prior attestations via the existing `compute_validity` path.
findings.finding: `registry_version()` (used in `claim_gate_check` invalidation via `compute_validity`) hashes only the PACKAGED `criteria_routing.json` + `CANONICAL_DET`/`CANONICAL_LLM`/`CODEBASE_GROUNDED` — it does NOT include the project's `.rebar/` routing overlay. The AC requires 'Activating/editing/disabling a project criterion invalidates a prior plan-review attestation at claim time', but under the current `registry_version()` implementation a change to `.rebar/`'s overlay produces an identical hash, so `claim_gate_check` will NOT detect the change and a stale attestation (reviewed without the new/changed project criterion) will be accepted.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Activating/editing/disabling a project criterion invalidates a prior plan-review attestation at claim time'; also '## What exists vs. what we build — Net-new: Claim-gate attestation invalidation on criteria change'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an optional `repo_root` parameter to `registry_version(repo_root=None)`. When `repo_root` is given, hash the `.rebar/` overlay file (if present) into the basis dict alongside the packaged routing. Pass `repo_root` through from `claim_gate_check` → `compute_validity` → `registry_version`. This is a minimal, surgical change that reuses the existing `regver:` manifest line mechanism.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the cache invalidation/isolation strategy for `_routing_index()` and `load_criteria()` when project overlays are introduced — e.g., key the cache by `(repo_root, overlay_mtime)`, or split into a `_packaged_routing_index()` (process-level cached) + a `_project_overlay(repo_root)` (repo-keyed, not cached or short-TTL), and merge at `effective_criteria(repo_root)` call time.
findings.finding: The `_routing_index()` cache bug is correctly named, but the proposed fix (`effective_criteria(repo_root)` through the loader) does not specify the invalidation/isolation mechanism for the `@lru_cache(maxsize=1)` call. The current code calls `_config.repo_root()` dynamically inside `_descriptor_from_prompt` for the PROMPT path (so prompts are resolved correctly per-call), but `_routing_index()` is cached at process level — once populated, it never reflects a different repo's overlay regardless of what `repo_root` is passed. The plan names 'naive merge leaks one repo's routing to all' but does not state whether the fix is (a) make `_routing_index` accept and key by `repo_root`, (b) clear the cache on repo switch, or (c) skip the lru_cache for the overlay portion.
findings.location: ## What exists vs. what we build — 'Net-new (build)' bullet 1: 'the loaders are `@lru_cache`d with no repo key — a naive merge leaks one repo's routing to all'
findings.suggested_fix: Split the two concerns: keep `_routing_index()` as `@lru_cache(maxsize=1)` for the PACKAGED routing (immutable for a given binary), and introduce `_project_overlay(repo_root)` with `@lru_cache(maxsize=None)` keyed by `(repo_root, mtime_of_overlay_file)` — or simply uncached since the overlay file is small. `effective_criteria(repo_root)` merges both. This preserves the performance benefit of caching the large packaged index while never leaking a project overlay across repos.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `.rebar/` routing overlay schema: enumerate its allowed fields (e.g., `block_threshold`, `default_posture`, `disabled`), their types/value vocabularies, and how they diff/patch against the built-in `criteria_routing.json` descriptor — co-locate this with (or reference) the existing descriptor schema in `registry.py`.
findings.finding: The .rebar/ routing overlay schema — the central data structure 3 ACs depend on — has its field vocabulary nowhere defined in the plan. AC2 says 'routing/threshold in a .rebar/ overlay (namespaced, additive)'; AC3 says 'the overlay may re-tune or disable a built-in'; AC7 says 'project-configured threshold/posture.' But the plan never specifies which fields the overlay contains (is it 'block_threshold'? 'default_posture'? a 'disabled: true' flag?), what their allowed value types are, or how the overlay is structured relative to the existing criteria_routing.json format. The design principle says 'reuse existing formats' and references '.rebar/plan_review_moves.json additive-merge precedent', but plan_review_moves.json carries {move_id: {name, template, applies_when?}} — structurally unlike a criteria-routing override. Without the overlay field vocabulary, an implementer cannot build the loader, the attestation invalidation check, or the 'disable recorded in attestation' feature.
findings.location: Design Decision 6; AC2; AC3; AC7; 'What exists vs. what we build' — routing overlay bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Define the overlay as a JSON object keyed by criterion id, where each entry is a partial `criteria_routing.json` descriptor (same field names, subset allowed). Add a `disabled: bool` field (not in the current descriptor) to express AC3's disable. Document namespace enforcement: project criterion ids must NOT match any key in `CANONICAL_LLM | CANONICAL_DET` (enforced in `effective_criteria(repo_root)` at load time, raising `RegistryError` on collision).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `effective_criteria(repo_root)` extends (or replaces) `registry_version()` to include project-criteria-overlay changes, and state whether adding a project-criteria hash to the signed manifest requires a payload-version bump — resolving whether prior certified attestations (which lack this field) are silently invalidated or gracefully treated as 'no project criteria were active'.
findings.finding: The plan's AC8 ('Activating/editing/disabling a project criterion invalidates a prior plan-review attestation at claim time') asserts this requires adding a 'regver/effective-criteria check' to claim_gate_check, but the existing attest.registry_version() function (already used in the drift-refresh path at attest.py:364) stamps only CANONICAL_DET + CANONICAL_LLM + the packaged criteria_routing.json — all built-in-only. Project criteria loaded from .rebar/ are outside CANONICAL_LLM and outside the packaged routing index, so they would NOT be captured by the current registry_version() hash. The plan does not specify how effective_criteria(repo_root) would extend registry_version() to cover project criteria, nor whether adding a project-criteria hash to the signed manifest would require a payload-version bump (analogous to the verified_at_sha binding concern surfaced in other plans on this codebase). Without this specification, the implementer cannot know whether (a) a supplementary project-hash field is added to the manifest, (b) registry_version() is replaced with a repo_root-keyed effective_criteria_version(), or (c) the existing regver stamp is extended in place — each of which has different blast-radius for existing certified attestations.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC8 ('Activating/editing/disabling a project criterion invalidates a prior plan-review attestation at claim time'); 'What exists vs. what we build' section — 'Claim-gate attestation invalidation on criteria change (claim_gate_check never re-compares the routing version — must add a regver/effective-criteria check)'; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:37-55 (registry_version), attest.py:364 (manifest_regver check in drift-refresh)
findings.suggested_fix: The lowest-blast-radius option: define `effective_criteria_version(repo_root)` that hashes `registry_version()` + a sorted representation of the .rebar/ routing overlay (if present). Treat a missing project-overlay field in an existing manifest as 'no project criteria were active at review time' (not a version mismatch), so prior built-in-only attestations remain valid. New attestations stamp both the built-in regver and the project overlay hash. This requires no payload-version bump and no prior-attestation invalidation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how a built-in criterion disable is recorded in the attestation: define the manifest line format (e.g., `disabled: G6,T8`) and whether the disabled-criteria set is folded into `registry_version()` (it must be, to trigger invalidation at claim time when the disabled set changes).
findings.finding: The 'disable recorded in attestation' feature (AC3) has no specified manifest field or schema extension. The existing attestation manifest in attest.py uses a line-based format (plan-review: PASS/FAIL, material: ..., regver: ..., dep ..., blocking: N, advisory: N); there is no 'disabled_criteria' field or equivalent. The plan says 'a disable is recorded in the attestation coverage' but never defines the manifest line(s) that carry this information, making the invalidation logic at claim time (how does claim_gate_check know a criterion was disabled since signing?) and the coverage report (what does 'recorded' mean exactly?) un-implementable.
findings.location: AC3; 'What exists vs. what we build' — claim-gate attestation invalidation bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add the disabled-criteria set as a sorted, comma-delimited `disabled:` line in the manifest (written by `build_manifest`), and fold it into the `registry_version()` hash basis so a change to disabled criteria changes the regver stamp and forces a full re-review at claim time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the enforcement mechanism for the 'project criterion cannot rebind a built-in' invariant: name the check's location (e.g., in `effective_criteria(repo_root)`), the comparison set (`CANONICAL_LLM | CANONICAL_DET`), and the error behavior (raise `RegistryError` with the colliding id).
findings.finding: The namespace enforcement rule that prevents a project criterion from rebinding a built-in (stated as a safety invariant in AC2 and Design Decision 6) has no specified enforcement mechanism. The plan says 'a project id can't rebind a built-in' but never defines what counts as a collision (exact-id match? prefix match?) or where the check lives. The existing prompt_library.py uses `_CRITERION_PREFIX = 'plan-review-'` so built-in criterion prompt ids are like `plan-review-T8`; a project criterion authored at `.rebar/prompts/plan-review-T8.md` would currently override a built-in via the existing `get_prompt` project-wins resolution (prompts.py). The plan says 'can never rebind' but the existing machinery actively permits it via the project-override path — meaning a new enforcement gate is required, but its location and logic are unspecified.
findings.location: AC2; Design Decision 6; 'What exists vs. what we build' — effective_criteria bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the documentation AC an explicit commitment to document the `.rebar/` routing overlay schema (key names, value types, namespacing rules, merge semantics, and disable semantics) — either as a reference doc or a JSON Schema file colocated with the loader.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new `.rebar/` routing overlay (namespaced, additive, with per-criterion threshold/posture keys) as a new config contract/schema, but the documentation AC only mentions 'how a project authors/activates/evals/blocks on a criterion' — it does not explicitly commit to documenting the overlay schema/format spec (key names, value types, validation rules, merge semantics).
findings.location: AC line 10: 'Docs: how a project authors/activates/evals/blocks on a criterion'; 'Design decisions' #6: 'Routing/threshold in a `.rebar/` overlay (merged over built-ins...; namespaced + additive)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `rebar/llm/criteria/overlay_schema.json` (JSON Schema) that validates the overlay format, and reference it from the user-facing authoring doc. The schema itself serves as both validation and documentation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a RED/probe fixture or unit test that exercises the multi-repo cache isolation property of the repo_root-keyed loader fix before full build-out (spin up two distinct repo_root values, verify each gets its own routing table and changes to one do not bleed into the other).
findings.finding: The @lru_cache repo_root keying fix is asserted to prevent cross-repo routing leakage, but no probe, unit test, or RED fixture is called out to verify the fix actually works and doesn't degrade hot-path performance — correctness of this subtle concurrency/caching property cannot be established by reasoning alone.
findings.location: What exists vs. what we build — 'uncached, repo_root-keyed loaders; the loaders are @lru_cache'd with no repo key — a naive merge leaks one repo's routing to all'
findings.suggested_fix: Write a parametrized unit test: construct two temporary repo roots each with a different .rebar/ overlay, call effective_criteria() for both in the same process, assert the returned criterion sets are independent and that mutating one overlay does not affect the cached result for the other.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the per-criterion eval runner extends `eval_solver.run_case` for plan-review-criterion prompt IDs — e.g., does it instantiate a fixture ticket and re-enter the plan-review gate, or use a lighter direct single-criterion invocation path? (AC6 depends on this design decision.)
findings.finding: The plan asserts a 'per-criterion eval runner' as net-new work but does not specify how the eval_solver invocation model extends to project-criteria prompt IDs. Today eval_solver.run_case hard-codes exactly three prompt_ids (completion-verifier, ticket-quality, spec-alignment) and raises ValueError for anything else. A plan-review criterion eval is a fundamentally different invocation shape (it runs the plan-review gate on a fixture ticket, not a verifier/ticket-quality op), so the adapter design is non-trivial and unspecified.
findings.location: What exists vs. what we build — 'A per-criterion eval runner (eval is per-prompt whole-suite; the live solver is a closed hardcoded dispatch; the CLI is validate-only)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the eval runner dispatch protocol for project LLM criteria: define how `eval_solver.run_case()` is extended (e.g., a generic plan-review-criterion solver path for all `plan-review-<cid>` prompt ids), what the expected `.rebar/evals/<id>.eval.yaml` fixture shape is, and what the fallback is when no solver entry exists for a given criterion id.
findings.finding: The per-criterion eval runner is described as net-new ('the live solver is a closed hardcoded dispatch; the CLI is validate-only') but the plan never specifies the dispatch protocol for project criteria — specifically, how `eval_solver.run_case()` (currently raising `ValueError` for unknown prompt_ids, confirmed at eval_solver.py:112) is extended to handle arbitrary project criterion ids, or what the fallback behavior is when a project criterion's eval fixture exists but no solver entry does. The plan's AC6 promises 'The eval/discrimination harness + a calibration/agreement view are surfaced per-criterion (CLI)' without specifying the eval invocation shape for a project criterion (does it use the same `rebar prompt eval <id>` CLI? does it require a `.rebar/evals/<id>.eval.yaml`? what is the solver entry point?).
findings.location: AC6; 'What exists vs. what we build' — per-criterion eval runner bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add a generic criterion-eval solver branch in `run_case()` that handles any `plan-review-<cid>` prompt id by running it through the existing plan-review finder pipeline with the case's plan text as input, reusing the existing `plan-review-finder` case shape. Document that project criteria use the same `.eval.yaml` format as built-in plan-review criteria.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the ironclad fail-open posture with LLM blocking criteria: explicitly state the error-path behavior (e.g., LLM blocking criteria fall back to a warning/advisory on API error, preserving fail-open), and add it to AC7 or a new sub-criterion.
findings.finding: The 'fail-open (ironclad)' posture binding constraint is reconciled for DET criteria (AC4: 'fail-open when the engine binary is absent') but never reconciled for LLM blocking criteria — no section specifies what happens when an LLM criterion is configured to block but its LLM call errors.
findings.location: Posture (inherited, binding) vs. Design Decision 3 / AC7
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC7: 'On LLM evaluation error, a blocking LLM criterion degrades to advisory (fail-open), and the attestation records the degraded coverage.' This mirrors the DET fail-open rule in AC4 and upholds the ironclad posture.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a rollback/grace-period mechanism for attestation invalidation on criteria change (e.g., a migration step that seeds an initial regver from current config on first upgrade, and a `--force` or `--accept-criteria-change` escape hatch at claim time analogous to the existing `--force`).
findings.finding: The plan adds attestation invalidation when criteria change ('Activating/editing/disabling a project criterion invalidates a prior plan-review attestation at claim time'), which is a behavioral breaking change for any workflow that stores and re-presents attestations, but no rollback or grace-period mechanism is described.
findings.location: AC line 8: 'Activating/editing/disabling a project criterion invalidates a prior plan-review attestation at claim time'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Spike the live preview endpoint before building the editor UI: stand up a minimal prompt-execution endpoint, measure round-trip latency for a representative criterion prompt, and verify the editor's request/response model can accommodate it (or decide on async/streaming).
findings.finding: The live must-fire/must-not-fire preview endpoint for the visual editor is identified as net-new ('no prompt-execution endpoint exists today'), but no spike or prototype is called out to validate that a synchronous prompt-execution endpoint can be safely exposed in the editor's request/response model without timeout, resource, or security issues.
findings.location: What exists vs. what we build — 'Editor live must-fire/must-not-fire preview (no prompt-execution endpoint exists today)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate (by file and function) the existing callsites of CANONICAL_LLM and the plan-review criteria_routing.json loader that must be migrated to rebar.llm.criteria, so no callsite is silently left on the old path.
findings.finding: When the unified rebar.llm.criteria registry replaces the existing plan-review CANONICAL_LLM frozenset and packaged-only criteria_routing.json, the specific callers that depend on those constructs are not enumerated, leaving migration scope implicit and risking missed callsites.
findings.location: What exists vs. what we build — Net-new bullet: 'Gate actually runs an authored criterion — today it is listable-but-inert (CANONICAL_LLM frozenset + packaged-only criteria_routing.json)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the backward-compatibility contract for existing signed attestations when the regver/effective-criteria field is added: specify whether missing-field attestations are treated as legacy-valid, auto-invalidated, or trigger an error, and describe an expand-contract rollout (add nullable field → deploy check in warn-mode → enforce) so no deployed claim is broken mid-migration.
findings.finding: The plan changes the signed attestation format (adding regver/effective-criteria fields) and the claim-gate check logic, but provides no backward-compatibility or expand-contract strategy for existing attestations that lack the new fields.
findings.location: Net-new (build) — 'Claim-gate attestation invalidation on criteria change'; AC: 'Activating/editing/disabling a project criterion invalidates a prior plan-review attestation at claim time'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an expand-contract migration note: (1) ship claim_gate_check with regver treated as optional/nullable — missing = legacy-valid (no regression); (2) new attestations always include regver; (3) once all live attestations carry regver (or have a known TTL), enforce the field. Document that old attestations without regver are treated as covering only built-in criteria (safe-fallback), not as an error.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the calibration/agreement view output schema: specify the fields rendered per criterion (at minimum: criterion id, recall, false-accept rate, Cohen's kappa or agreement %, fixture count), the CLI invocation (`rebar eval calibrate <criterion-id>`?), and the output format (table/JSON).
findings.finding: The 'calibration/agreement view' is described as net-new ('metrics exist; rendering is net-new') but the plan never defines the output schema or CLI surface the rendering produces — not the fields shown (e.g., criterion id, recall, false-accept, Cohen's kappa, agreement %), not the grouping (per-gate? per-criterion? aggregate?), and not the format (JSON? table? JUnit XML?). A gate consumer building the calibration view cannot know what to render without this specification.
findings.location: AC6; Design Decision 7; 'What exists vs. what we build' — calibration view bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for the registry unification (e.g., a feature-flag or expand-contract sequence that keeps the per-gate registries functional until the unified layer is validated in production).
findings.finding: The plan describes 'replacing the two parallel registries' (plan-review + b744 code-review) with a unified `rebar.llm.criteria` layer, but provides no rollback path or expand-contract sequencing. If the unified registry misbehaves post-migration, both gates are simultaneously broken with no stated back-out mechanism.
findings.location: AC line 1: 'A shared `rebar.llm.criteria` registry that both plan-review and code-review consume, replacing the two parallel registries'; 'What exists vs. what we build' section
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce an expand-contract sequence: (1) add `rebar.llm.criteria` as an *additive* layer that each gate optionally delegates to while retaining its own registry as fallback; (2) validate unified routing in both gates; (3) then remove the per-gate registries. Document the back-out step: revert the delegation call and re-enable the per-gate registry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite AC6 as a specific observable outcome, e.g.: `rebar criteria eval <criterion-id>` prints per-criterion discrimination score, Cohen's kappa, and pass/fail calibration threshold to stdout; exit 0 on success.
findings.finding: AC6 ('eval/discrimination harness + calibration/agreement view are surfaced per-criterion (CLI)') is effort-framed — 'surfaced' is not a specific observable outcome; no CLI command, flag, or output format is specified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'The eval/discrimination harness + a calibration/agreement view are surfaced per-criterion (CLI); FP-replay (F1) is explicitly out of scope'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite the Docs AC as a specific observable outcome: name the target file(s) (e.g., `docs/project-criteria.md`), list the required sections (authoring, activation, eval, blocking, invariant model), and state a CI/in-session check (e.g., file exists and contains the section headings).
findings.finding: AC10 ('Docs: how a project authors/activates/evals/blocks on a criterion; the invariant = blocking-criterion model') is effort-framed — 'Docs' names a work type, not a specific observable outcome; no target file path, required section headings, or completeness test is given.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Docs: how a project authors/activates/evals/blocks on a criterion; the invariant = blocking-criterion model'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or explicitly de-scope) the routing derivation/validation pipeline: either 'A routing generator produces criteria_routing.json from source-of-truth config and a drift gate detects manual edits,' or move it to Out of Scope with a rationale.
findings.finding: The 'routing derivation/validation pipeline' is described as a net-new deliverable in 'What exists vs. what we build' but no Acceptance Criterion covers it.
findings.location: 'What exists vs. what we build' (Net-new) vs. Acceptance Criteria

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion (or explicitly defer/descope) for the routing derivation/validation pipeline so every net-new item listed in 'What exists vs. what we build' has an observable end-state proof.
findings.finding: The 'routing derivation/validation pipeline' is listed as explicit net-new work in the design section ('criteria_routing.json is hand-maintained; no generator/drift gate today') but has no corresponding Acceptance Criterion or end-state-observable proof, making it unclear whether an implementer should build it and how to confirm it is done.
findings.location: What exists vs. what we build — Net-new bullet: 'A routing derivation/validation pipeline'
findings.suggested_fix: Either add an AC such as 'A generator/drift-check gate derives criteria_routing.json from source-of-truth; a CI step fails if the checked-in file diverges' or move the pipeline to Out of Scope with an explicit rationale.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
